author: [{'author': "The author is a textual element representing a full name, typically displayed in a prominent and distinct manner, often in a larger font size compared to other text elements. It is centrally positioned on the page, usually near the top or center, and is associated with the authorship of a document, such as an academic thesis or dissertation. The name is clearly distinguished from other text elements like titles, abstracts, committee information, and is not part of a list or tabular format. It is written in a natural language font, distinct from code or non-textual elements, and is often followed by degree information and the institution name. The author's name is not part of a bibliography, reference section, table of contents, or blocks of text describing experimental results or observations, which are typically in smaller font sizes and positioned within the body of the document. The name is a standalone element, not accompanied by lists of tables or other non-author related information, and is not a title or heading within the document body."}]
chapter: [{'chapter': 'A chapter is a primary organizational element in academic and formal documents, serving as a major division that introduces and structures new topics or sections. It is typically positioned at the beginning of a new section and is distinguished by a larger font size compared to the main text but smaller than the document title. The chapter is often labeled with the word "Chapter" followed by a number or Roman numeral, and a title that can be in all capital letters or sentence case. The title may vary in length, from a single word to a multi-line description, and can optionally include a subtitle for additional context. Chapters function to organize content and are visually set apart from the main body text, bibliographic citations, appendix sections, subsections, tables, and table captions. They are distinct from document titles, which are usually larger and may include author and context details, and from subsection headers, which are smaller and lack the "Chapter" label. Chapters are typically separated from the main text and other elements like figures and captions, emphasizing their role as key structural components.'}]
equation: [{'equation': 'An equation is a structured mathematical expression that consists of variables, constants, operators, and functions, arranged to represent a mathematical relationship or computation. It is characterized by a clear layout with terms and operators aligned for readability, often including symbols such as plus, minus, equals, and others. Equations may also incorporate specialized notations like matrices, integrals, or derivatives. They are distinct from plain text, tables, and lists due to the presence of mathematical symbols and the absence of grid structures or linear text arrangements. Equations serve to express precise mathematical relationships, derive expressions, or compute values, and are typically embedded within a larger context of mathematical or scientific discourse.'}]
equation number: [{'equation number': "The 'equation number' class refers to small, distinct numerical labels enclosed in parentheses or directly adjacent to mathematical equations within academic and scientific texts. These numbers serve as references for the equations, facilitating easy citation and location within the document. They are sequentially assigned and appear in close proximity to the equations they reference, without being part of the main text flow. Equation numbers are specifically formatted to be easily distinguishable from the surrounding text and are not part of any procedural or functional content within the document. They are visually smaller and less prominent than section headings, which are larger, bolded text that introduces new sections of the document and are part of the main text flow. Equation numbers are distinct from other numerical elements, such as page numbers, section headings, or footnote markers, which are typically part of a larger block of text and are not directly associated with mathematical equations or variables. Additionally, equation numbers are not part of the mathematical expressions themselves, such as subscripts or coefficients, which are directly associated with variables within the equations. They are crucial for maintaining the structure and coherence of scientific and mathematical documents, ensuring that readers can easily navigate and reference the equations or variables that support the text's theoretical and practical discussions. Equation numbers can also appear in various formats, such as being enclosed in parentheses or directly adjacent to the equation, and can be located on the right or left side of the page, depending on the document's layout. Furthermore, equation numbers are not located at the bottom of the page as page numbers are, and they are not part of the main text flow, unlike section headings."}]
figure: [{'figure': 'A visual representation used to convey complex information in a clear and understandable manner, primarily through graphical elements. This includes a wide range of visual types such as line charts, heatmaps, process diagrams, and chemical structure diagrams. Figures use specific visual elements like axes, data points, lines, numerical scales, legends, arrows, text boxes, labels, and molecular symbols to represent data trends, comparisons, processes, and chemical structures. They are distinct from textual elements such as headings, paragraphs, lists, tables, or footnotes and are essential for visualizing and explaining intricate information across various domains. Figures specifically exclude lists of figure captions, isolated alphanumeric codes without accompanying visual elements, tabular data organized into rows and columns, and hierarchical lists such as tables of contents. The class includes data visualization figures, which are characterized by their use of graphical elements to represent and analyze data trends, including numerical scales and error bars, and are often accompanied by descriptive captions that explain the context and significance of the data. Figures also encompass process diagrams, which use diagrams, arrows, and labels to illustrate sequences of steps or workflows, enhancing understanding by presenting information in a structured and visual format.'}]
figure caption: [{'figure caption': 'The "figure caption" class encompasses textual elements that are directly associated with and positioned immediately adjacent to specific visual representations such as images, graphs, diagrams, or equations. These captions include a figure number, a concise title, and a detailed description of the visual data, providing context, explaining the purpose and key findings, and enhancing the reader\'s understanding. They are closely integrated with the surrounding visual content and use appropriate labels, legends, annotations, or explanatory text to ensure clarity and precision. A true instance of the "figure caption" class is characterized by its direct association with a specific visual representation, its inclusion of a figure number and a concise title, and its detailed description of the visual data. In contrast, other textual elements in the document, such as headings, section titles, footnotes, mathematical equations, or lists of figure captions that do not provide detailed descriptions and are not immediately adjacent to the visual content, do not serve this explanatory role. Additionally, tables presenting numerical data without a figure number, title, or detailed description of visual content are distinct from figure captions. The caption should be closely integrated with the visual content and use appropriate labels, legends, annotations, or explanatory text to ensure clarity and precision, and it should provide a comprehensive explanation of the visual data.'}]
footnote: [{'footnote': 'A footnote is a textual annotation positioned at the bottom of a page, separate from the main body of text, and begins with a superscript number that corresponds to a reference in the main text. It provides additional information, explanations, or references related to a specific part of the document. The content is concise and specific, offering citations, explanations, definitions, or other relevant details that support or expand upon the main text. Footnotes maintain a uniform style and format throughout the document, enabling readers to quickly recognize and access supplementary information. They are visually distinct from other elements by their placement and use of superscript numbers, and are not part of the main content, graphical representations, lists of figures or tables, bibliographies, subheadings, or annotations directing to external sources.'}]
list of content heading: [{'list of content heading': 'Titles that introduce lists of tables, figures, abbreviations, or similar content within documents. These titles are consistently placed at the top of a page, formatted in a bold, larger font compared to the rest of the text on the page, and explicitly state "List of Tables," "List of Figures," "List of Abbreviations," or similar phrases. The content that follows includes a structured list of tables, figures, abbreviations, or related items with corresponding page numbers, which can be either a single entry or multiple entries, depending on the type of content being listed. These titles are distinct from other text elements such as chapter headings, section titles, figure captions, footnotes, continuous blocks of text, bibliographic references, and mathematical equations embedded within text. They do not contain detailed discussions or explanations related to the content of the document and are not located at the bottom of the page or serve as page numbers. Additionally, these titles are not part of a continuous block of text that includes detailed explanations, ensuring they are easily distinguishable from section headings or other content within the document. They are specifically designed to provide an overview and quick access to the listed content, making them essential for document navigation. The titles are also not part of the actual content, such as tables or data, but serve as introductory elements to the lists of content. They are not part of a table of contents or any hierarchical structure of section titles and page numbers. Furthermore, these titles are followed by structured lists of tables, figures, abbreviations, or related items with corresponding page numbers, which is a key distinguishing feature. The titles are formatted in a bold, larger font compared to the rest of the text on the page and are explicitly stated to introduce the specific type of content they are associated with, such as "List of Tables," "List of Figures," or "List of Abbreviations."'}]
list of content text: [{'list of content text': 'A structured and formatted list serving as a navigational aid in documents, featuring a consistent layout with uniform spacing, alignment, and font style. It includes either a hierarchical enumeration of chapters, sections with associated page numbers, or a sequential listing of figures, tables with descriptive titles and corresponding page numbers. This list is distinct from continuous narrative text, bibliographic references, footnotes, and other non-navigational elements. It is designed to facilitate easy access to different sections or specific figures and tables within the document, typically placed at the beginning. The list does not contain detailed data entries or comparisons, differentiating it from content tables or figures. It can be categorized into a table of contents for document structure and a list of figures or tables for visual elements.'}]
page number: [{'page number': "The page number is a small, clear, numerical value consistently located either at the bottom center or bottom right corner of the page, separate from the main content. It may or may not be enclosed within a square or any other shape, and is presented in a simple font. It is sequential, providing a clear indication of the page's position within a larger document. The page number is distinct from other elements such as titles, headings, tables, and main content due to its placement and simplicity, ensuring it does not interfere with the main content while still serving its purpose effectively. It is not a name or any other form of text that is larger, more prominent, or located in the middle of the page. It is not part of a title or heading, which are typically larger, bolder, and located at the top of the page. Additionally, the page number is not a mathematical equation or any other complex text that contains symbols, variables, or operators. It is not enclosed in parentheses or any other form of punctuation, and it is not part of a mathematical equation or reference within the main content of the page. Furthermore, the page number is not a graphical element or a complex visual representation such as a histogram or chart. It is also not a footnote or any other form of additional text that provides supplementary information related to the main content. The page number is not part of a table of contents or any other list that includes descriptive phrases alongside numerical values."}]
paragraph: [{'paragraph': "A paragraph is a structured block of text that forms a coherent unit of thought within a document. It consists of multiple sentences that are logically connected and contribute to a unified theme or argument. Typically, paragraphs are written in a consistent font size and style, maintaining a continuous narrative flow. They are designed to inform, educate, or engage the reader by conveying detailed information, often incorporating data, statistics, and references. Paragraphs are distinct from titles, headings, and figure captions, which have different structural and functional characteristics. They are also separate from tables of contents, which list sections and page numbers without a continuous narrative. The physical configuration of a paragraph includes a block of text that is aligned with the document's margins, maintaining a uniform layout and spacing throughout the text."}]
reference text: [{'reference text': "The 'reference text' class encompasses a list of bibliographic entries that are integral to academic and scientific documents. These entries are characterized by a structured format that includes authors, publication years, titles, and other publication details, presented in a serif font and separated by line breaks. The content is specific and detailed, providing the necessary information to locate the source material, which can range from journal articles, books, conference papers, and technical reports. The entries may also include URLs or DOIs for direct access to the source. The consistent and standardized format, along with the focus on providing detailed source information, distinguishes this class from other textual elements in a document. The visual characteristics, such as the serif font and the separation of entries by line breaks, further help in accurately identifying true instances of 'reference text' while minimizing false positives, such as lists of tables, figure captions, or other textual elements that do not follow the structured format and detailed content typical of bibliographic entries. The 'reference text' class does not include experimental results, figures, or other non-bibliographic content, ensuring that it is distinct from continuous blocks of descriptive text, mathematical notations, and equations. Each entry in the 'reference text' class includes authors, publication years, titles, and other publication details, and may be separated by line breaks, further distinguishing it from sections that contain discussion questions, participant details, or other non-bibliographic information, which are typically found in appendices or other parts of a document. The 'reference text' class is specifically designed to provide a comprehensive and standardized way to cite sources, ensuring that readers can easily locate and access the original material. The use of a serif font and line breaks between entries aids in the visual identification of this class, reducing the likelihood of confusion with other types of text. The 'reference text' class also includes URLs or DOIs for direct access to the source, which is an important feature for readers to easily access the original material."}]
section: [{'section': 'A section is a distinct part of a document introduced by a bold, larger, and numbered text element, which serves as a section heading. This heading is part of a hierarchical structure, indicated by the numbering, and is followed by a block of text that elaborates on the topic. The content within a section is formatted in a standard paragraph style and can include text, images, and mathematical equations. Sections are distinct from other elements such as the table of contents, bibliographic entries, tables, and page numbers. The section heading is a key visual characteristic that distinguishes a section from other blocks of text in the document, and the content within a section is elaborative and not merely informational or introductory like a title page.'}]
subsection: [{'subsection': 'A subsection heading is a bolded and larger text that appears within the body of a document, following a logical or numerical hierarchy to indicate a subtopic under a larger section. It is positioned at the beginning of a new subsection and is consistently formatted to stand out from the surrounding text, usually in a larger font size and bold typeface. This heading is part of a structured outline that helps readers navigate the document and is distinct from other elements such as figures, tables, mathematical equations, or bibliographic references. Crucially, a subsection heading is followed by a paragraph that elaborates on the content, distinguishing it from other headings or titles that may introduce different types of content, such as simulation studies or methodological descriptions. The subsection heading is not a table caption, a structured data entry, or part of a table of contents but a textual element that provides a clear and concise introduction to the subtopic, ensuring it is not confused with captions or other non-textual elements. Additionally, a subsection heading is not a title of a document or a chapter heading, which are typically placed at the top of a page and followed by additional metadata or a list of contents.'}]
subsubsection: [{'subsubsection': "A subsubsection is a bolded, numerically prefixed heading that introduces a specific topic or research proposition within a larger section of a document. It is typically located at the beginning of a new section or paragraph and is followed by related content, such as a detailed explanation or discussion. The heading is concise and descriptive, summarizing the main topic or hypothesis of the subsequent text. It is distinct from other text elements, such as tables, bibliographic references, page numbers, footnotes, or non-title content, due to its role in organizing the document's structure at a more granular level. Subsubsections are used to provide a clear outline and guide readers through the document, making it easier to navigate and understand the content. The subsubsection is not located in the middle of a paragraph and is not associated with mathematical equations or footnotes. It is also not a general heading for a section that lists tables and their corresponding page numbers, such as a table of contents. Additionally, a subsubsection is not a hypothesis statement, which is typically bolded and italicized and introduces a specific research proposition within the body of the text. Unlike a section heading, a subsubsection is either a single word or numerically prefixed and is used to introduce a more specific topic or research proposition within a larger section of the document, rather than a broader topic. Furthermore, a subsubsection can also be a bolded heading that introduces a specific research hypothesis or proposition, providing a clear and concise summary of the subsequent content. This type of subsubsection is distinct from other text elements and is used to guide readers through the document's structure, making it easier to navigate and understand the content. The subsubsection is also characterized by its location at the beginning of a new section and its numerical prefix, which helps to distinguish it from other bolded headings that may appear in the middle of a paragraph or lack a numerical prefix."}]
table: [{'table': 'A table is a structured arrangement of data organized in a grid format consisting of rows and columns. It typically includes headers at the top of each column to label the data type or category. Tables may also feature subheaders, titles, and footnotes to provide additional context and information. The content within a table can consist of numerical values, text, or a combination of both, systematically aligned to facilitate easy reading and comparison. Tables are designed to present and analyze data in a clear, organized manner, often used in research, reports, and studies. They are distinct from continuous text, single numerical values, and lists without a grid structure, and are characterized by their systematic layout and the relationship between rows and columns. Tables can include various elements such as mathematical notations, comparative data, and legends for visual elements, enhancing the understanding and interpretation of the information presented.'}]
table caption: [{'table caption': "A table caption is a textual element that provides a detailed description and context for a table. It is typically positioned directly above or below the table and includes a title that begins with 'Table' followed by a number and a colon, succinctly summarizing the table's content. The caption may extend to multiple lines and contains a thorough explanation of the table's components, such as methodology, conditions, results, and other relevant details. It is formatted as a continuous block of text, distinct from section headings or general text blocks, and is directly associated with the table it describes. The table caption is more comprehensive than a simple title, offering additional information about the table's content and structure, and is not to be confused with lists of chapter titles, section headings, or standalone labels."}]
table of contents text: [{'table of contents text': 'A structured and organized layout of a document\'s sections, including hierarchical numbering for chapters, sections, and subsections, with descriptive titles summarizing the content of each part. The sections contain page numbers aligned to the right for easy navigation, and may be connected by dotted lines. The text is in a serif font, with uniform font size and style for readability and a professional appearance. The layout is consistent, featuring a vertical list of sections with clear indentation levels, and it does not contain detailed data, statistical measures, body text content, mathematical expressions, or inline references. The table of contents text may include higher-level sections like "Chapter" or "Part" to group related sections, and it can span multiple pages while maintaining a consistent format throughout. The inclusion of a "Contents" heading at the beginning of the table of contents text further clarifies its purpose and structure. The table of contents text specifically excludes detailed descriptions of simulation results, settings, and experimental setups, which are more characteristic of body text or scientific content. It may also include sections such as "References," "Publications," and "Vita," which are not typically found in the main body of the document. Additionally, the table of contents text does not contain detailed figure descriptions or technical terms specific to the content of the figures, which are more characteristic of a list of figures or tables. The table of contents text is a structured and organized layout of a document\'s sections, including hierarchical numbering for chapters, sections, and subsections, with descriptive titles summarizing the content of each part. The sections contain page numbers aligned to the right for easy navigation, and may be connected by dotted lines. The text is in a serif font, with uniform font size and style for readability and a professional appearance. The layout is consistent, featuring a vertical list of sections with clear indentation levels, and it does not contain detailed data, statistical measures, body text content, mathematical expressions, or inline references. The table of contents text may include higher-level sections like "Chapter" or "Part" to group related sections, and it can span multiple pages while maintaining a consistent format throughout. The inclusion of a "Contents" heading at the beginning of the table of contents text further clarifies its purpose and structure. The table of contents text specifically excludes detailed descriptions of simulation results, settings, and experimental setups, which are more characteristic of body text or scientific content. It may also include sections such as "References," "Publications," and "Vita," which are not typically found in the main body of the document. Additionally, the table of contents text does not contain detailed figure descriptions or technical terms specific to the content of the figures, which are more characteristic of a list of figures or tables.'}]
title: [{'title': "The title of an academic document is a single line of text that is prominently placed at the very top of the page, written in a larger and bolder font size compared to the rest of the text, and is centrally aligned. It succinctly summarizes the main topic or research focus of the entire document and is the first piece of information presented, setting the context for the whole document. The title is distinct from other elements such as tables of contents, which contain multiple lines of text and serve as navigational aids. The title is typically followed by the author's name, the degree being pursued, the institution, and the committee members, all of which are formatted in a smaller font size. This format is consistent across various disciplines and types of academic documents, making it easy to recognize and differentiate from other elements in the scene. The title is distinct from section headings, which are also written in a larger and bolder font but are located within the body of the document and introduce specific sections rather than summarizing the entire document. Additionally, the title is not a graphical element, mathematical equation, part of a series of equations, explanatory text within the body of the document, or a footnote providing additional information or citation details. The title is also distinct from lists or tables of contents, which contain multiple lines of text and serve as navigational aids. The title is also distinct from page numbers, which are small, simple numerical values located at the bottom of the page and do not summarize any content. The title is a single-line text that serves as the main heading of the document and is not part of the document's content, nor does it contain bibliographic information such as authors, publication titles, journal names, and URLs. The title is also distinct from tables, which contain multiple lines of text and numerical data organized into columns and rows, and table captions, which are short phrases or sentences that describe the content of a table and are written in a smaller font size and not centrally aligned."}]
